analogy: Think of "Synthetic Fibres and Plastics" through the chapter's own opening images: COAL AND PETROLEUM COAL AND PETROLEUM Can air, water and soil be exhausted by human activities? You have already studied about water in Class VII. In the light of the availability of various resources in nature, natural resources can be broadly classified into two kinds:... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: COAL AND PETROLEUM COAL AND PETROLEUM, 3.1 Coal, COAL AND PETROLEUM 33, SCIENCE 34. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Synthetic Fibres and Plastics is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that cOAL AND PETROLEUM COAL AND PETROLEUM Can air, water and soil be exhausted by human activities?

The text explains that you have already studied about water in Class VII.

The text explains that in the light of the availability of various resources in nature, natural resources can be broadly classified into two kinds: (i) Inexhaustible Natural Resources: These resources are present in unlimited quantity in nature and are not likely to be exhausted by human activities.

The text explains that (ii) Exhaustible Natural Resources: The amount of these resources in nature is limited.

The text explains that examples of these resources are forests, wildlife, minerals, coal, petroleum, natural gas etc.

The text explains that fill them with popcorn/peanuts/roasted gram/toffees.

The text explains that further divide each group into three subgroups containing 1, 2 and 4 students.

The text explains that label them as first, second and third generation respectively.

The text explains that we use various materials for our basic needs.

The text explains that some of them are found in nature and some have been made by human efforts.

The text explains that natural Man-made Can we use all our natural resources forever ?

The text explains that does this list include air, water, soil and minerals?

The text explains that since all these are obtained from nature, they are called natural resources.

The text explains that as population is growing, second and third generations have larger number of consumers.

The text explains that put one full container for each group on a table.

The text explains that ask consumers of the first generation from each group to consume eatables from the container of their group.

The text explains that assume that the eatables in the c o n t a i n e r r e p r e s e n t t h e t o t a l availability of an exhausible natural resource like coal, petroleum or natural gas.

The text explains that in this chapter we will learn about some exhaustible natural resources like coal, petroleum and natural gas.

The text explains that about 300 million years ago the earth had dense forests in low lying wetland areas.

The text explains that due to natural processes, like flooding, these forests got buried under the soil.

The text explains that the temperature also rose as they sank deeper and deeper.

The text explains that under high pressure and high temperature, dead plants got slowly converted to coal.

The text explains that as coal contains mainly carbon, the slow process of conversion of dead vegetation into coal is called carbonisation.

The text explains that 3.2: A coal mine Coal Gas Coal gas is obtained during the processing of coal to get coke.

The text explains that now a days, it is used as a source of heat rather than light.

The text explains that coal is processed in industry to get some useful products such as coke, coal tar and coal gas.

The text explains that cOAL AND PETROLEUM 35 as a fuel in many industries situated near the coal processing plants.

The text explains that petrol and diesel are obtained from a natural resource called petroleum.

The text explains that the word petroleum is derived from petra (rock) and oleum (oil) as it is mined from between the rocks under Earth as shown in Fig.

The text explains that over millions of years, absence of air, high temperature and high pressure Refining of Petroleum Petroleum is a dark oily liquid.

The text explains that wells Gas Oil Water Reservoir rock Impervious rock 2024-25

The text explains that natural gas is stored under high pressure as compressed natural gas (CNG).

The text explains that the great advantage of CNG is that it can be used directly for burning in homes and factories where it can be supplied through pipes.

The text explains that 3.4 Some Natural Resources are Limited You have studied in the beginning of the chapter that some natural resources are exhaustible like fossil fuels, forests, minerals etc.

The text explains that their tips are l drive at a constant and moderate speed as far as possible, l switch off the engine at traffic lights or at a place where you have to wait, l ensure correct tyre pressure. l ensure regular maintenance of the vehicle.

The text explains that their formation is a very slow process and conditions for their formation cannot be created in the laboratory.

The text explains that sCIENCE 38 KEYWORDS KEYWORDS KEYWORDS KEYWORDS KEYWORDS COAL COAL GAS COAL TAR COKE FOSSIL FUEL NATURAL GAS PETROLEUM PETROLEUM REFINERY WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNT WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNT WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNT WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNT WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNT ‹ Coal, petroleum and natural gas are fossil fuels. ‹ Fossil fuels were formed from the dead remains of living organisms millions of years ago. ‹ Fossil fuels are exhaustible resources. ‹ Coke, coal tar and coal gas are the products of coal. ‹ Petroleum gas, petrol, diesel, kerosene, paraffin wax, lubricating oil are obtained by refining petroleum. ‹ Coal and petroleum resources are limited.

The text explains that (b) Process of separation of different constituents from petroleum is called .

The text explains that explain why fossil fuels are exhaustible natural resources.

The text explains that explain the process of formation of petroleum.

The text explains that what could be the reasons for their being located at those places?

The text explains that plot shortage percentage for the years on the Y-axis and the year on the X-axis.

The text explains that year Shortage (%) 1 1991 7.9 2 1992 7.8 3 1993 8.3 4 1994 7.4 5 1995 7.1 6 1996 9.2 7 1997 11.5 E X E R C I S E S Extended Learning — Activities and Projects 1.

The text explains that mark the places in the map where coal, petroleum and natural gas are found.

The text explains that show the places where petroleum refineries are situated.

The text explains that choose any five families of your neighbourhood.

The text explains that enquire whether their energy consumption (coal, gas, electricity, petrol, kerosene) has increased or decreased in the last five years.

The text explains that enquire also about the measures they adopt to conserve energy.

The text explains that find out the location of major thermal power plants in India.

The text explains that for more information, visit: l www.energyarchive.ca.gov l web.ccsd.k12.wy.us l web.pcra.org 2024-25

Source: NCERT Class 8 Science, Chapter 3: Synthetic Fibres and Plastics

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Synthetic Fibres and Plastics, which statement is supported by the source text about COAL AND PETROLEUM COAL AND PETROLEUM?
options: COAL AND PETROLEUM COAL AND PETROLEUM Can air, water and soil be exhausted by human activities?
In the light of the availability of various resources in nature, natural resources can be broadly classified into two kinds: (i) Inexhaustible Natural Resources:...
(ii) Exhaustible Natural Resources: The amount of these resources in nature is limited.
Examples of these resources are forests, wildlife, minerals, coal, petroleum, natural gas etc.
